title: Night Shift: Acute Psychosis in Young Adult
scenarioIntro: A 22-year-old male is found by family acting paranoid, hallucinating, and refusing care at 02:00. Crew must manage safety, de-escalation, and transport, following Ontario mental health protocol.

callInformation.dispatchNotes: Young male, hallucinating, refusing care
callInformation.crewNotes: Night shift psychosis. Prioritize safety, de-escalation, and mental health assessment.

incidentNarrative: Crew arrives to find a 22-year-old pacing, talking to unseen people, and refusing to let anyone approach. Family is anxious and frightened.
patientPresentation: Standing, agitated, talking to self, suspicious, avoids eye contact.

evolvingFindings: Agitation worsens if confronted; risk of violence

transportPhase.handoffConsiderations: Communicate mental status, safety concerns, and family input.

sample.eventsLeadingUp: Recent stress, then gradual change

caseProgression.withProperTreatment: Patient de-escalated, agrees to transport, no violence.
caseProgression.withoutProperTreatment: Agitation escalates, risk of harm to self/others.
caseProgression.withIncorrectTreatment: If confronted or restrained, violence or elopement occurs.

protocolNotes: Ontario BLS/ALS PCS mental health protocol: Scene safety, de-escalation, transport if possible.
Night shift calls may have increased agitation and unpredictability.
teachersPoints: Acute psychosis requires calm de-escalation, scene safety, and mental health assessment.
clinicalReasoning: This patient is experiencing acute psychosis. Early de-escalation and protocol-driven care are critical.
scenarioRationale: Teaches psychosis management, de-escalation, and scene safety.
learningObjectives: Recognize acute psychosis
Prioritize scene safety and de-escalation
Communicate with anxious family
Escalate care per protocol
selfReflectionPrompts: How did you ensure scene safety?
What clues pointed to psychosis?
How did you de-escalate the situation?
What would you do differently if agitation escalated?

3: Recognizes agitation but underestimates violence risk.
Misses family anxiety as a factor.
Delays de-escalation.
5: Identifies psychosis and safety risk.
Maintains calm, manages family, and plans for escalation.
Balances scene control with clinical care.
7: Anticipates rapid escalation and leads a coordinated de-escalation response.
Integrates scene, physiologic, and social factors.
Maintains high awareness of subtle changes and adjusts care dynamically.

3: Obtains only a partial story from family.
Misses timeline and prior stressors.
Relies on patient for answers despite paranoia.
5: Uses family to clarify timeline, symptoms, and prior health.
Confirms no drug use and identifies gradual onset.
Integrates collateral history into risk assessment.
7: Extracts a concise, high-value timeline despite scene stress.
Uses family support efficiently to clarify risk and guide care.
Integrates history directly into mental health and transport decisions.

3: Performs a basic assessment but incompletely trends agitation and risk status.
Misses the significance of hallucinations as a red flag.
Reassessment is inconsistent.
5: Performs structured mental health and risk assessment.
Uses serial reassessment to track improvement or worsening.
Recognizes hallucinations as a warning sign and escalates care.
7: Builds a coherent assessment from mental health, risk, and scene context.
Detects subtle changes early and adjusts plan proactively.
Maintains high-quality reassessment cadence.

3: Removes family from room but delays de-escalation.
Anchors on drug use as cause rather than psychosis.
Transport decision is delayed or not adjusted after persistent agitation.
5: Keeps scene safe, initiates de-escalation, and plans for escalation.
Plans rapid transport and keeps family informed.
Adjusts care plan based on reassessment.
7: Executes a decisive, well-sequenced plan prioritizing de-escalation, safety, and transport.
Anticipates escalation and prepares for escalation before instability occurs.
Leads team and family in a coordinated, high-quality response.

3: Provides basic updates but does not clearly explain urgency to family.
Role allocation during management is inconsistent.
Handoff omits key mental health and trend details.
5: Communicates clearly with family about mental health, de-escalation, and transport plan.
Keeps family informed and calm.
Delivers organized handoff with timeline, mental health, and response.
7: Uses calm, directive communication to coordinate care in a stressful night setting.
Maintains closed-loop communication across all phases.
Provides a concise, high-value handoff for mental health management.

title: Night Shift: Violent/Agitated Patient in Police Custody

evolvingFindings: Agitation worsens if not calmed; risk of sudden cardiac arrest

title: Night Shift: Pediatric Drowning at Community Pool
scenarioIntro: A 6-year-old is pulled from a community pool unresponsive at 20:45. Crew must manage airway, hypoxia, and family distress, following Ontario trauma protocol.

callInformation.dispatchNotes: Child unresponsive, drowning, lifeguard present

title: Nighttime Cough and Collapse
scenarioIntro: This scenario focuses on pediatric respiratory distress with sudden deterioration at night, emphasizing croup/upper airway management, scene control with anxious caregivers, and Ontario BLS/ALS PCS-compliant escalation and transport decisions.

callInformation.dispatchNotes: 3-year-old with barking cough, stridor, sudden collapse, parents panicked
callInformation.hazardsOrFlags: Dim lighting, anxious parents, tight bedroom space
callInformation.crewNotes: Night shift pediatric airway call. Prioritize airway assessment, minimize agitation, and escalate per protocol.

incidentNarrative: Crew is dispatched overnight for a 3-year-old who developed a barking cough and noisy breathing, then suddenly became limp and less responsive. Parents report the child had a mild cold for two days but worsened suddenly after midnight. The home is dimly lit and the family is highly anxious.
patientPresentation: Child is lying on the bed, pale, with inspiratory stridor and intermittent barking cough. He is drowsy, minimally responsive, and retracting with each breath. Parents are panicked and repeatedly attempt to rouse him.

patientDemographics.chiefComplaint: Sudden collapse after cough and noisy breathing

sceneArrival.sceneEnergy: High stress, low light, anxious caregivers

evolvingFindings: Stridor worsens with agitation; drowsiness increases if not managed promptly

transportPhase.handoffConsiderations: Communicate timeline, airway interventions, response to oxygen, and trend in mental status.

opqrst.provocation: Worse with agitation, improves with calm and cool air

caseProgression.withoutProperTreatment: If agitated or left untreated, airway swelling worsens, stridor increases, and child may become unresponsive.
caseProgression.withIncorrectTreatment: If forced to lie flat or agitated, airway obstruction worsens rapidly.

expectedTreatment: Minimize stimulation and keep child upright
Prepare for airway escalation if deterioration occurs
Transport promptly with ongoing airway monitoring

Night shift calls may have delayed presentation and higher caregiver anxiety

title: Seizure in the Basement
scenarioIntro: This scenario focuses on pediatric seizure management at night, emphasizing post-ictal reassessment, scene navigation in a cluttered basement, and Ontario BLS/ALS PCS-compliant escalation and transport decisions.

callInformation.hazardsOrFlags: Dim lighting, clutter, stairs, anxious parents
callInformation.crewNotes: Night shift pediatric seizure call. Prioritize airway, post-ictal trend, and safe movement.

incidentNarrative: Crew is dispatched overnight for a 6-year-old who had a witnessed seizure in a cluttered basement bedroom. The child is now post-ictal, drowsy, and confused. Parents are present and anxious. The basement is dimly lit and cluttered, complicating movement.

caseProgression.withIncorrectTreatment: If moved unsafely or airway not protected, risk of injury or aspiration increases.

title: Asthma Attack at the Sleepover
scenarioIntro: This scenario focuses on pediatric asthma exacerbation at night during a sleepover, emphasizing medication access, scene navigation with multiple children, and Ontario BLS/ALS PCS-compliant escalation and transport decisions.

callInformation.hazardsOrFlags: Crowded living room, multiple children, missing medication
callInformation.crewNotes: Night shift pediatric asthma call. Prioritize medication access, scene control, and escalation per protocol.

incidentNarrative: Crew is dispatched late evening for an 8-year-old at a sleepover who developed severe wheezing and shortness of breath. The inhaler cannot be found. Multiple children and caregivers are present, creating a chaotic scene.
patientPresentation: Child is sitting upright, using accessory muscles, and speaking in short phrases. He is anxious, diaphoretic, and wheezing audibly. Other children are watching and caregivers are searching for the inhaler.

sceneArrival.sceneEnergy: Chaotic, crowded, anxious caregivers and children

caseProgression.withIncorrectTreatment: If child is forced to lie flat or not reassured, distress and hypoxia worsen.

protocolNotes: Ontario BLS PCS pediatric asthma protocol: Bronchodilator as soon as possible, oxygen as tolerated, monitor for fatigue
Night shift calls may have increased scene chaos and medication access issues
teachersPoints: Nighttime pediatric asthma calls require rapid medication access, scene control, and escalation planning. Fatigue and hypoxia are red flags. Ontario BLS PCS compliance is essential.
clinicalReasoning: This child is experiencing an asthma exacerbation at night with missing medication. Early bronchodilator, oxygen, and scene control are critical. Ontario BLS PCS compliance is required.

title: The Tight Stairwell Wheeze
scenarioIntro: This scenario focuses on advanced respiratory distress assessment, evidence-based bronchodilator treatment, continuous reassessment, and movement planning in a challenging environment. The patient’s respiratory status will improve with treatment but may worsen with exertion during stair movement if not managed according to Ontario BLS/ALS PCS 3.4/5.4.
incidentNarrative: Crew is dispatched to a second floor apartment for a male with shortness of breath. On arrival, patient is found sitting on a couch leaning forward in tripod position. He is speaking in short sentences and appears anxious. Audible wheezing is heard from across the room. Patient states he has asthma and forgot his inhaler at work. He reports symptoms started about an hour ago after cleaning a dusty storage room and have gradually worsened. Scene is complicated by narrow stairs and warm environment.
patientPresentation: Patient sitting upright leaning forward, using accessory muscles. Audible wheezing present. Patient appears anxious and speaks in short sentences. Skin slightly diaphoretic. Accessory muscle use and fatigue are evident.

sceneArrival.accessIssues: Second floor access via narrow stairs

firstImpression.apparentSeverity: Potentially time-sensitive, risk of rapid deterioration

initialAssessment.generalImpression: Asthma exacerbation, risk of respiratory failure
immediatePriorities: Oxygen titration as per BLS PCS

additionalHistory: Previous ICU admission for asthma (if asked)

keyFindings: Improved SpO2 and air entry after bronchodilator
evolvingFindings: Risk of recurrent distress with movement or fatigue

transportConsiderations: Slow stair movement with rest breaks
Continuous reassessment for fatigue or silent chest
Monitor SpO2 and respiratory effort
ongoingCare: Repeat bronchodilator as per ALS PCS if needed
transportPhase.handoffConsiderations: Convey response to treatment, risk of deterioration during movement, and need for ongoing reassessment.

opqrst.provocation: Worse with movement, talking, and exertion
opqrst.quality: Chest tightness with wheeze and fatigue
opqrst.time: Gradually worsened over the past hour

sample.signsAndSymptoms: Shortness of breath, wheezing, chest tightness, anxiety, fatigue
sample.eventsLeadingUp: Cleaning dusty storage room, then progressive worsening

physicalExam.generalAppearance: Anxious, tripod position, fatigued
physicalExam.breathing: Rapid, labored, wheezes bilaterally, fatigue evident
physicalExam.chest: Wheezes throughout lung fields, decreased air movement, fatigue present

secondSet.context: After first bronchodilator and positioning of comfort

additionalSets.context: After rapid stair movement without rest (deterioration)
After second bronchodilator during transport (improvement)

withProperTreatment: After first bronchodilator and upright positioning, work of breathing decreases and SpO2 improves.
Patient can speak in longer sentences with less anxiety.
With controlled movement and reassessment, patient remains stable to ED handoff.
withoutProperTreatment: If movement occurs before treatment, distress worsens quickly with exertion.
RR rises, SpO2 falls, and fatigue progresses to reduced responsiveness.
Risk escalates to respiratory failure during transport if not corrected.
withIncorrectTreatment: If the patient is laid supine or moved aggressively, dyspnea and anxiety worsen.
Delayed bronchodilator leads to poorer response and persistent wheeze.
Failure to reassess trends increases risk of missed deterioration.

expectedTreatment: Primary assessment as per BLS PCS
Bronchodilator administration as per ALS PCS
Dexamethasone administration as per ALS PCS
Reassessment after bronchodilator and after movement
Plan slow controlled movement with rest breaks
Monitor SpO2, respiratory effort, and fatigue
Consider repeat bronchodilator as per protocol
Transport with ongoing reassessment and monitoring for fatigue/silent chest
protocolNotes: Follow BLS PCS 3.4 for respiratory distress and asthma management
Administer bronchodilator and dexamethasone as per ALS PCS 5.4 if indicated
Monitor for fatigue and silent chest as red flags for respiratory failure
Reassess after each intervention and after movement
Movement may worsen respiratory distress; treat before moving whenever possible
Oxygen titration as per protocol; avoid hyperoxia if SpO2 is adequate
teachersPoints: Scene constraints (stairs, space, bystander pressure) should change movement and communication strategy. Respiratory patients often worsen with movement; treat before moving whenever possible. Reassessment after bronchodilator is critical and should include lung sounds, respiratory effort, and fatigue. A quieter chest in an asthma patient can indicate worsening airflow and impending respiratory failure. Respiratory calls are dynamic; temporary improvement does not remove deterioration risk.
clinicalReasoning: This patient is experiencing an asthma exacerbation likely triggered by environmental dust exposure. Bronchospasm causes airway narrowing leading to wheezing, increased work of breathing, and air trapping. Bronchodilators should improve airflow and reduce work of breathing. Movement increases oxygen demand and work of breathing and may worsen symptoms. Quiet chest, fatigue, or decreasing respiratory effort would indicate worsening condition and possible impending respiratory failure. Ontario BLS/ALS PCS compliance is required throughout.
scenarioRationale: This scenario is designed to teach students the importance of reassessment after treatment, the impact of movement on respiratory patients, and planning patient packaging and movement carefully. Students should recognize that treatment timing and movement planning significantly affect patient condition. The scenario also reinforces that respiratory calls are dynamic and require ongoing reassessment throughout the call and transport. Ontario BLS/ALS PCS compliance is emphasized.
learningObjectives: Recognize asthma exacerbation and risk of respiratory failure
Administer bronchodilator appropriately as per ALS PCS
Reassess after treatment and after movement
Understand how movement affects respiratory patients
Recognize signs of respiratory fatigue and silent chest

How did movement affect the patient?
What signs would indicate this patient is deteriorating?
What would you do differently next time?
When should you repeat bronchodilator treatment?
How would you recognize fatigue or silent chest?

5: Performs respiratory assessment including breath sounds and work of breathing, reassesses after movement and roommate arrival.

Uses assessment findings to guide further treatment and movement planning.

title: The Short Walk to the Bathroom
scenarioIntro: This scenario focuses on geriatric trauma assessment, suspected pelvic fracture, pain management, movement planning, and recognition of occult bleeding with shock progression. Ontario trauma and shock protocols are emphasized throughout.

dispatchNotes: Elderly female fall, cannot get up
callInformation.crewNotes: Movement may worsen injuries and hypotension; follow BLS/ALS PCS trauma and shock principles.

incidentNarrative: Crew is dispatched for an elderly female who fell while walking to the bathroom during the night. On arrival, patient is found lying on the bedroom floor beside the bed. She reports falling onto her right side and now has severe hip and pelvic pain with inability to stand. She denies head strike but reports severe pain with any attempt to move. The scene is complicated by low lighting, a narrow doorway, and limited working space around the bed.
patientPresentation: Patient is lying side-lying on the floor, holding her right hip and pelvis. She appears pale, diaphoretic, and very uncomfortable. She reports severe pain with movement and resists repositioning. The right leg appears shortened and externally rotated.

sceneArrival.sceneDescription: Small bedroom with limited space beside the bed
sceneArrival.accessIssues: Narrow doorway complicates packaging

firstImpression.generalAppearance: Elderly patient in pain with stable airway but concerning pallor
firstImpression.apparentSeverity: Possible pelvic fracture with internal bleeding risk

initialAssessment.circulation: Radial pulse present and slightly rapid
initialAssessment.generalImpression: Suspected pelvic fracture with occult bleeding risk
immediateInterventions: IV access if indicated per ALS PCS

bystanderInformation: Patient fell while walking to the bathroom
contradictionsOrBarriers: Pain limits movement and detailed assessment
sceneContextClues: No obvious wet floor or environmental cause identified

secondaryAssessment.generalAppearance: Pain remains severe and pallor is more noticeable after movement
secondaryAssessment.breathing: Still non-labored but discomfort increases with packaging
secondaryAssessment.circulation: Tachycardia increases and blood pressure trends downward after movement
evolvingFindings: Hypotension risk increases if moved without stabilization

transportConsiderations: Expedite transport to appropriate trauma destination
Minimize movement and maintain pelvic stabilization
ongoingCare: Fluid and pain management per protocol if indicated
transportPhase.handoffConsiderations: Include mechanism, anticoagulation status, pelvic stabilization, vital sign trend, and any shock progression during packaging and transport.

opqrst.time: Immediate after the fall and constant since

sample.signsAndSymptoms: Hip pain, inability to bear weight, dizziness with attempted movement
sample.eventsLeadingUp: Walking to the bathroom overnight, tripped and fell beside the bed

physicalExam.generalAppearance: Elderly female lying on the floor in significant pain
physicalExam.circulation: Radial pulse present and slightly rapid
physicalExam.abdomen: Soft with mild lower abdominal tenderness
physicalExam.pelvis: Pain with gentle compression, instability suspected
physicalExam.extremities: Right leg shortened and externally rotated

secondSet.context: After initial packaging and reassessment

additionalSets.context: After movement to stretcher without ideal stabilization
During transport after stabilization and reassessment

withProperTreatment: With careful movement and pelvic stabilization, pain remains severe but hemodynamics decline more slowly.
Serial vital signs reveal early shock features without abrupt collapse.
Rapid transport and reassessment keep the patient stable enough for trauma handoff.
withoutProperTreatment: If shock risk is underestimated, tachycardia and hypotension progress during packaging and transport.
Skin becomes cooler and paler as occult bleeding worsens.
Delayed recognition of the trend increases risk of decompensation before hospital arrival.
withIncorrectTreatment: If the patient is encouraged to stand or is moved aggressively, pain spikes and hemodynamics worsen quickly.
Poor stabilization increases bleeding risk and accelerates shock progression.
Failure to trend vitals after movement can miss a major deterioration point.

expectedTreatment: Primary assessment as per BLS PCS
Minimize movement and stabilize the pelvis if indicated
Pain assessment and management per protocol
Monitor for occult bleeding and shock
Trend vital signs before and after movement
protocolNotes: Follow BLS PCS 3.4 for trauma and shock management
Suspected pelvic fracture should prompt movement minimization and stabilization
Anticoagulated geriatric trauma patients have elevated bleeding risk
Vital sign trends after movement may reveal occult shock earlier than a single set
Rapid transport should take priority over prolonged scene time once immediate stabilization is addressed
teachersPoints: Pelvic fractures can hide significant internal bleeding before obvious collapse. Geriatric and anticoagulated patients may compensate poorly and deteriorate after movement. Packaging and movement decisions are part of treatment, not just logistics. Trend vitals after movement rather than relying on the first reassuring set. Rapid transport matters once stabilization and shock recognition are underway.
clinicalReasoning: This patient likely has a pelvic fracture after a low-mechanism geriatric fall. Pelvic injury combined with anticoagulation creates a meaningful risk of occult internal bleeding and progressive shock. Pain, inability to bear weight, pelvic tenderness, and a shortened externally rotated leg support serious injury rather than a simple soft tissue fall. Worsening tachycardia and falling blood pressure after movement suggest the patient is losing compensation. Ontario BLS/ALS PCS compliance is required throughout.
scenarioRationale: This scenario is designed to teach recognition of geriatric pelvic trauma, the effect of movement on bleeding risk, and early shock recognition through trending rather than isolated vitals. It reinforces that packaging, stabilization, and transport decisions can materially change the patient’s trajectory.
learningObjectives: Recognize signs of pelvic fracture and occult bleeding risk
Understand how geriatric trauma and anticoagulation increase shock risk
Use movement planning and stabilization to reduce harm
Interpret vital sign trends for developing shock
Prioritize timely trauma transport with reassessment

How did the vital sign trend change your transport urgency?
What would you do differently in packaging this patient?
When does shock recognition become more important than additional on-scene time?

title: He Was Just Playing Hockey
scenarioIntro: This scenario focuses on the management of a witnessed cardiac arrest in a public setting. The call emphasizes high-performance CPR, early defibrillation, airway management decisions, rhythm recognition, medication timing, team leadership, and post-ROSC reassessment. Scene control and communication with bystanders matter early and continuously.

dispatchNotes: Male collapsed suddenly, not breathing, CPR in progress by bystanders
callInformation.crewNotes: AED reportedly applied before arrival; prioritize high-performance arrest management per BLS/ALS PCS.

incidentNarrative: The patient had just finished a recreational hockey game and was walking toward the arena lobby when he suddenly collapsed. Bystanders report no warning beyond mild shortness of breath after exertion. Collapse was witnessed, CPR was started within about 1 minute, and an on-site AED delivered one shock before paramedic arrival. The wife arrives during resuscitation and provides additional medical history while the lobby remains crowded and emotionally charged.
patientPresentation: Adult male lying supine on the arena lobby floor with bystander CPR already in progress. AED pads are in place. The patient is cyanotic, unresponsive, and apneic. Multiple bystanders are watching closely, one is trying to explain the collapse, and staff confirm the AED already advised and delivered a shock.

sceneArrival.sceneDescription: Arena lobby floor with CPR in progress and AED pads already applied
sceneArrival.accessIssues: Open floor but crowd control is needed to maintain workspace
sceneArrival.bystandersPresent: Multiple bystanders present; wife arrives during resuscitation

firstImpression.generalAppearance: Adult male in active cardiac arrest with bystander CPR underway
firstImpression.apparentSeverity: Immediate resuscitation required
initialRedFlags: Crowded scene may disrupt CPR flow

initialAssessment.airway: No advanced airway initially; airway opens with BVM and adjuncts
initialAssessment.generalImpression: Witnessed sudden cardiac arrest with early bystander CPR and AED use

bystanderInformation: AED delivered one shock before EMS arrival
contradictionsOrBarriers: Crowded loud scene makes collateral history fragmented
sceneContextClues: Public setting with extra rescuers available

secondaryAssessment.generalAppearance: Initially pulseless and cyanotic, then modest color improvement after ROSC
secondaryAssessment.breathing: Assisted ventilations continue; spontaneous effort begins after ROSC
secondaryAssessment.circulation: Pulseless during arrest; organized perfusing rhythm after successful resuscitation
keyFindings: Ventricular fibrillation on initial rhythm checks
ETCO2 rise accompanies improving resuscitation effectiveness and ROSC
missedIfNotAssessed: Low ETCO2 suggesting poor CPR quality
Early ROSC cues during pulse and rhythm checks
evolvingFindings: ROSC achieved with weak initial perfusion and ongoing rearrest risk

transportConsiderations: Rapid transport to cardiac-capable centre
Prepare for possible rearrest during movement
transportPhase.handoffConsiderations: Communicate witnessed collapse timeline, bystander CPR, AED shock before arrival, paramedic shocks and medications, ROSC timing, ETCO2 trend, and post-ROSC status.

opqrst.onset: Sudden collapse while walking off the ice
opqrst.provocation: Occurred just after exertion from hockey
opqrst.time: Approximately 6 to 8 minutes before paramedic arrival

sample.eventsLeadingUp: Finished playing hockey, walked toward lobby, then collapsed suddenly

physicalExam.generalAppearance: Unresponsive adult male with CPR in progress

firstSet.context: Initial paramedic rhythm check during arrest

secondSet.context: After continued CPR and next rhythm analysis

additionalSets.context: After further shock and medication cycle with improving CPR effectiveness
ROSC achieved

withProperTreatment: High-quality CPR is maintained with early defibrillation and minimal interruptions.
ETCO2 gradually rises as compressions improve and rhythm management stays organized.
The patient converts to a perfusing rhythm with ROSC, though post-ROSC instability remains a concern.
withoutProperTreatment: If CPR quality is poor or shocks are delayed, ETCO2 remains low and VF persists longer.
The rhythm may deteriorate to a less salvageable arrest state and ROSC becomes less likely.
Delayed organized care worsens neurologic and survival odds despite a witnessed arrest.
withIncorrectTreatment: If CPR is paused unnecessarily for airway tasks or disorganized rhythm checks, perfusion time is lost.
Delayed or unsafe defibrillation prolongs VF and reduces the benefit of an otherwise favorable witnessed arrest.
Failure to transition properly into post-ROSC care increases the risk of immediate rearrest.

expectedTreatment: Rapid rhythm recognition and defibrillation for VF
Airway adjuncts and effective BVM ventilations
IV or IO access as indicated
Cardiac arrest medications per ALS PCS
Organized rhythm and pulse checks every 2 minutes
protocolNotes: Follow BLS PCS for high-performance CPR and minimal interruption principles
Defibrillate ventricular fibrillation promptly per ALS PCS cardiac arrest algorithm
Airway interventions should not create prolonged CPR pauses
ETCO2 trends can help judge CPR effectiveness and support ROSC recognition
Post-ROSC care should focus on oxygenation, ventilation, perfusion, and rearrest surveillance
teachersPoints: Witnessed arrest plus early CPR and AED use creates a salvageable but time-sensitive scenario. Defibrillation timing matters more than perfect airway setup in early VF arrest. ETCO2 is useful for CPR quality coaching and ROSC recognition, not just documentation. Scene leadership must include role assignment, pause control, and bystander management. ROSC is not the end of the case; rearrest risk and post-ROSC instability need immediate attention.
clinicalReasoning: This scenario represents a witnessed sudden cardiac arrest most consistent with ventricular fibrillation of likely cardiac origin after exertion. Early bystander CPR and AED use preserve a meaningful chance of ROSC, but survival still depends on prompt defibrillation, uninterrupted compressions, and organized ALS arrest management. Rising ETCO2 and conversion to a perfusing rhythm support improving resuscitation effectiveness. Once ROSC occurs, the clinical focus must shift immediately to oxygenation, perfusion, neurologic status, and rearrest risk. Ontario BLS/ALS PCS compliance is required throughout.
scenarioRationale: This scenario is designed to teach witnessed VF arrest management in a public environment where bystander care has already started. It reinforces rhythm-driven priorities, high-performance CPR, clear team leadership, and the transition from arrest care to post-ROSC management under scene pressure.
learningObjectives: Demonstrate high-quality CPR and organized arrest sequencing
Recognize and manage a shockable cardiac arrest rhythm
Manage airway and ventilations without excessive CPR interruption
Use ETCO2 and reassessment to judge arrest progress and ROSC
Lead a resuscitation scene with clear communication and role control
selfReflectionPrompts: How did you protect CPR quality during rhythm checks, airway tasks, and shocks?
Did you prioritize defibrillation appropriately within the first cycles of care?
What cues suggested ROSC had occurred, and how quickly did you adapt?
How effective was your communication with partner, bystanders, and family during the arrest?
What would you tighten up on your next witnessed VF arrest call?

3: Recognizes cardiac arrest but loses track of the bigger scene picture once treatment begins, allowing crowd pressure or bystander noise to disrupt flow.
Sees VF on the monitor but is slow to appreciate how favorable witnessed-collapse timing and early AED use make defibrillation urgency even more important.
Misses subtle deterioration or improvement cues such as ETCO2 trend changes, AED history relevance, or the transition point from arrest care to ROSC care.
5: Immediately frames the case as a witnessed shockable arrest and keeps CPR quality, shock timing, and rhythm reassessment as the central priorities.
Maintains awareness of scene crowding, bystander involvement, AED history, and family arrival without losing focus on the resuscitation sequence.
Recognizes key shifts in the call, including improving ETCO2, rhythm change, and the need to pivot quickly into post-ROSC management.
7: Anticipates the next critical steps before each cycle ends, preparing the team for rhythm checks, charging, shocks, medications, and the possibility of ROSC or rearrest.
Uses scene information dynamically, incorporating bystander CPR quality, AED timing, and witness details into a strong prognosis-driven treatment tempo.
Tracks patient physiology, team performance, crowd control, and transport planning simultaneously without allowing any one domain to compromise another.

3: Obtains only fragmented collateral history and fails to clarify key facts such as witnessed status, CPR start time, or AED shock history.
Asks broad questions during the arrest but misses the highest-yield details that affect rhythm interpretation and resuscitation context.
Uses family input late or inefficiently, delaying useful background such as cardiac risk factors and exertional context.
5: Quickly confirms the most relevant arrest history: witnessed collapse, early CPR, AED shock delivered, and exertional lead-up.
Uses family and bystander information efficiently to identify likely cardiac etiology and approximate downtime without derailing active care.
Filters collateral information well, separating high-yield facts from emotional scene noise.
7: Extracts a concise, clinically powerful timeline while resuscitation continues, including collapse sequence, bystander actions, AED interval, and preceding symptoms.
Uses witness information to sharpen prognostic thinking and guide urgency without over-questioning the scene.
Integrates collateral history directly into treatment decisions, handoff quality, and post-ROSC destination framing.

3: Performs rhythm and pulse checks but allows them to become slow or poorly coordinated, creating avoidable pauses in compressions.
Notices monitor changes or ETCO2 values without fully translating them into meaningful arrest assessment decisions.
Reassessment after ROSC is delayed or shallow, with incomplete attention to perfusion, ventilations, and neurologic response.
5: Performs organized rhythm and pulse checks at appropriate intervals with good compression-pause discipline.
Uses ETCO2, monitor rhythm, perfusion signs, and patient response together to assess arrest progress and identify ROSC promptly.
Reassesses systematically after ROSC, including airway effectiveness, circulation, blood pressure, neurologic status, and rearrest risk.
7: Coordinates rapid, highly efficient reassessment at each cycle so that rhythm interpretation, pulse confirmation, ETCO2 trend, and clinical signs form one coherent picture.
Recognizes subtle but meaningful signs of improving perfusion before they become obvious to the rest of the team and adjusts priorities accordingly.
Transitions seamlessly from arrest assessment to post-ROSC reassessment without wasting time or repeating low-yield steps.

3: Understands the basic VF algorithm but allows airway tasks, role confusion, or medication timing issues to compete with the immediate need for shocks and compressions.
Makes broadly correct choices but in a sequence that creates unnecessary delays or poorly timed interventions.
After ROSC, hesitates to pivot away from the arrest algorithm and is slow to prioritize post-ROSC stabilization and transport.
5: Prioritizes CPR and defibrillation correctly for VF while integrating airway support and medications without major interruption cost.
Uses a sensible resuscitation sequence, anticipating rhythm checks, charging decisions, medication timing, and the need for post-ROSC care.
Recognizes the moment ROSC changes the clinical objective and adjusts management toward stabilization and rapid cardiac destination.
7: Demonstrates disciplined, algorithm-informed decision making that keeps every intervention in service of shockable-arrest survival rather than task completion.
Balances defibrillation, airway management, medication delivery, team coordination, and pause reduction with almost no wasted motion or indecision.
Makes strong forward-looking decisions after ROSC, anticipating hypotension, rearrest, ventilation issues, and transport needs before they become problems.

3: Gives commands during the arrest but they are intermittent, not clearly assigned, or not consistently confirmed, leading to avoidable confusion during shocks and rhythm checks.
Communicates with bystanders and family reactively rather than deliberately, allowing scene emotion to compete with team focus.
Provides an incomplete handoff that lists tasks performed but does not clearly tell the arrest story, timing, rhythm course, and ROSC transition.
5: Uses clear role assignment, timing cues, and rhythm-check language so the team knows when to compress, pause, charge, shock, ventilate, and reassess.
Communicates calmly with bystanders and family while protecting the resuscitation workspace and maintaining team concentration.
Delivers a structured handoff that includes witnessed status, bystander CPR, AED use, rhythm course, interventions, ROSC timing, and current stability.
7: Functions as a confident resuscitation leader with consistent closed-loop communication that keeps every team member synchronized through high-stress algorithm transitions.
Uses tone and word choice to control both the clinical team and the emotional scene, preventing crowd dynamics or family distress from degrading care.
Provides concise, high-value updates throughout the call and a final handoff that is clinically sharp, time-aware, and immediately actionable for the receiving team.

title: The Shaky Driver
scenarioIntro: This scenario focuses on assessment and management of altered level of consciousness in a diabetic patient. It emphasizes early blood glucose testing, recognition of hypoglycemia, dextrose administration, reassessment, and transport decision-making. The patient can improve quickly with correct treatment or deteriorate if the metabolic cause is missed.

dispatchNotes: Elderly male altered LOC, vehicle stopped on highway shoulder
callInformation.crewNotes: Suspect hypoglycemia given diabetic history; check BGL early and reassess after treatment.

incidentNarrative: Crew is dispatched for an elderly male found altered in his vehicle on the highway shoulder. Witnesses report he was driving erratically before pulling over and becoming less responsive. His wife, seated in the passenger seat, states he is diabetic, skipped lunch, and seemed shaky before becoming confused. She attempted oral glucose from his emergency kit, but he did not improve enough to safely continue driving or answer questions clearly.
patientPresentation: Patient is seated in the driver's seat, slumped slightly over the steering wheel. He is diaphoretic, confused, and slow to answer questions but responds to voice. His wife is anxious and trying to explain the events while traffic passes nearby.

sceneArrival.sceneDescription: Patient seated in stopped vehicle on highway shoulder
sceneArrival.accessIssues: Assessment begins in vehicle before safe extraction to stretcher

firstImpression.generalAppearance: Elderly male, altered but responsive, diaphoretic and medically unwell
firstImpression.apparentSeverity: Potentially reversible metabolic cause requiring prompt assessment

initialAssessment.generalImpression: Altered LOC likely from a metabolic cause
immediateInterventions: Prepare dextrose if BGL confirms hypoglycemia

bystanderInformation: Oral glucose was attempted without meaningful improvement
contradictionsOrBarriers: Patient confused and gives limited reliable history
sceneContextClues: Hot day and delayed meal increase metabolic stress

secondaryAssessment.generalAppearance: Noticeably more alert after dextrose with reduced diaphoresis
secondaryAssessment.breathing: Remains stable throughout treatment
secondaryAssessment.circulation: Heart rate improves as glucose normalizes
missedIfNotAssessed: Alternative causes of altered LOC if glucose correction fails
evolvingFindings: Mental status improves quickly with treatment but still requires reassessment and transport planning

transportConsiderations: Monitor for recurrent hypoglycemia
Ensure safe transfer from roadside environment
ongoingCare: Observation for recurrent confusion or decline
transportPhase.handoffConsiderations: Include initial glucose value, glucose treatment given, response timeline, and concern for recurrence based on insulin use and missed meal.

opqrst.onset: Gradual over the morning with more obvious decline this afternoon
opqrst.provocation: Missed meal, ongoing activity, and heat exposure

sample.eventsLeadingUp: Skipped lunch, continued driving in hot weather, became increasingly confused

physicalExam.generalAppearance: Elderly male who is confused, sweaty, and medically unwell

secondSet.context: After dextrose administration and reassessment

additionalSets.context: Thirty minutes after treatment during transport

withProperTreatment: BGL is checked early, hypoglycemia is confirmed, and dextrose is given promptly.
Confusion and diaphoresis improve rapidly with normalization of glucose.
The patient remains stable in transport with repeat glucose checks and no recurrent decline.
withoutProperTreatment: If glucose is not checked early, confusion worsens and the patient may become combative or less responsive.
Failure to identify the metabolic cause delays the only rapidly reversible intervention.
Ongoing decline increases the risk of airway compromise, seizure, or a more complex roadside extraction.
withIncorrectTreatment: If the case is treated as stroke or a vague altered-LOC complaint without checking BGL, the patient does not improve appropriately.
Wrong diagnostic focus delays dextrose and prolongs neuroglycopenia.
Failure to reassess after treatment can miss recurrence or an incomplete response.

expectedTreatment: Primary assessment and safe roadside management
Early BGL check in altered LOC
Dextrose administration if indicated per ALS PCS
Monitoring for recurrence or incomplete correction
Transport with continued glucose and neurologic monitoring
protocolNotes: Check BGL early in altered LOC patients per BLS PCS assessment priorities
Administer dextrose for confirmed hypoglycemia per ALS PCS
Reassess clinical response and repeat glucose after treatment
Do not anchor only on a metabolic diagnosis if response is incomplete; maintain differential thinking
Transport decisions should account for recurrence risk and insulin use even when the patient improves
teachersPoints: BGL is a high-yield early test in altered LOC and should not be delayed behind low-yield tasks. Hypoglycemia can mimic other neurologic or medical presentations, especially in older adults. Rapid improvement with dextrose is helpful but does not remove the need for reassessment and transport planning. Collateral history from family is often the key to recognizing a missed meal or insulin mismatch. A roadside diabetic emergency also requires scene safety and extraction planning, not just medication administration.
clinicalReasoning: This patient most likely has hypoglycemia causing altered level of consciousness, diaphoresis, shakiness, and impaired driving. Missed oral intake in the setting of diabetes treatment created a neuroglycopenic presentation that improved quickly after dextrose. The strong response to glucose supports hypoglycemia as the leading diagnosis, but reassessment remains important because altered LOC can also reflect stroke, infection, toxicologic causes, or other metabolic problems if recovery is incomplete. Ontario BLS/ALS PCS compliance is required throughout.
scenarioRationale: This scenario is designed to teach the importance of checking blood glucose early in altered LOC, treating confirmed hypoglycemia promptly, and reassessing both the patient and the differential after improvement. It also reinforces that scene safety, collateral history, and transport decisions remain important even when the patient recovers quickly.
learningObjectives: Recognize hypoglycemia as a time-sensitive cause of altered LOC
Perform early BGL assessment in a medical altered-LOC presentation
Administer dextrose appropriately and reassess response
Maintain a reasonable differential diagnosis if improvement is incomplete
Plan transport and monitoring for recurrence risk after initial correction
selfReflectionPrompts: Why was BGL such an important early test in this altered-LOC call?
What findings helped you prioritize hypoglycemia over other causes?
How did the patient’s response to dextrose change your working diagnosis?
What would make you worry that hypoglycemia is not the whole story?
What transport and reassessment issues still matter after apparent recovery?

3: Recognizes a medical altered-LOC problem but is slow to frame diabetes and missed oral intake as the most time-sensitive clues.
Focuses on general confusion management without appreciating the danger of a diabetic patient who was actively driving.
Underestimates how scene factors like highway traffic, roadside extraction, and anxious family input affect both urgency and workflow.
5: Identifies the combination of altered LOC, diaphoresis, diabetic history, and missed meal as strongly suggestive of hypoglycemia.
Maintains awareness of both the medical issue and the roadside safety problem while organizing assessment and movement.
Recognizes that rapid improvement after treatment is helpful but does not end the need for monitoring and transport planning.
7: Immediately frames the call around a likely reversible metabolic cause while still protecting against diagnostic anchoring if the response is incomplete.
Integrates patient presentation, wife’s history, diabetic supplies in the vehicle, and the unsafe driving context into one coherent priority plan.
Anticipates the next phase of care early, including recurrence risk, reassessment needs, and the implications of treating a diabetic emergency in a highway environment.

3: Obtains only a partial story and misses high-yield details such as skipped lunch, insulin use, or the limited benefit of the oral glucose already given.
Relies on the confused patient for answers that should be clarified through the wife or visible scene clues.
Asks broad questions but misses the timeline linking meal pattern, driving behavior, and mental status decline.
5: Uses the wife effectively to confirm diabetic history, medication use, missed meal, and the patient’s progression before the crew arrived.
Clarifies that oral glucose was attempted but did not produce enough recovery, supporting the need for further treatment and monitoring.
Builds a useful metabolic history without letting the interview delay care.
7: Extracts a concise but high-value timeline that links insulin use, missed intake, heat, driving impairment, and failed initial oral glucose response.
Uses collateral history and scene clues strategically, avoiding low-yield questioning while the patient is confused and vulnerable.
Integrates history directly into treatment urgency, reassessment planning, and transport risk framing.

3: Performs a general altered-LOC assessment but delays or de-emphasizes the BGL check despite clear diabetic clues.
Recognizes abnormal mentation and diaphoresis but does not fully connect them to a coherent metabolic picture.
Reassessment after dextrose is present but shallow, missing the opportunity to compare neurologic and glucose improvement clearly.
5: Performs an organized medical assessment that includes an early glucose check and focused neurologic reassessment.
Connects altered mentation, diaphoresis, tachycardia, and low BGL into a consistent diagnosis.
Uses post-treatment reassessment to confirm clinical improvement and identify the need for continued monitoring.
7: Builds a clear assessment from scene, symptoms, glucose data, collateral history, and serial response rather than treating the BGL as an isolated number.
Reassesses mental status, perfusion, and recurrence risk in a way that clearly distinguishes improvement from true resolution.
Maintains a strong parallel differential while still recognizing hypoglycemia as the most likely and urgent explanation.

3: Eventually treats the hypoglycemia but only after a delay caused by indecision or competing low-priority concerns.
Anchors too late on the glucose result or, conversely, treats the number without thinking through reassessment and recurrence risk.
Makes a transport plan that is technically acceptable but does not fully account for the possibility of recurrent symptoms or incomplete correction.
5: Checks glucose promptly, administers dextrose appropriately, and reassesses both symptoms and objective response.
Keeps a reasonable differential open in case recovery is incomplete or atypical.
Chooses transport and monitoring that fit the patient’s insulin use, roadside setting, and recent impaired driving episode.
7: Makes decisive, well-sequenced choices that prioritize the reversible metabolic emergency without losing sight of safety and alternate diagnoses.
Uses the treatment response intelligently, not just as proof of being right, but as one piece of the evolving clinical picture.
Plans beyond the immediate correction, anticipating recurrence, need for repeat glucose checks, and the implications of returning to baseline only temporarily.

3: Provides basic explanations but does not clearly guide the wife through what is happening or why glucose assessment and treatment are urgent.
Team updates during assessment and treatment are inconsistent, making the call feel reactive rather than organized.
Handover communicates that hypoglycemia was treated but misses the surrounding context of driving behavior, delayed meal, prior oral glucose attempt, or recurrence risk.
5: Explains the assessment and treatment plan clearly to the wife while keeping the confused patient calm and manageable.
Communicates the BGL result, treatment plan, and reassessment findings effectively within the team.
Provides a handoff that includes the metabolic concern, treatment response, and the reasons transport is still appropriate after improvement.
7: Uses calm, purposeful communication to control a roadside medical scene, reassure family, and maintain patient cooperation despite confusion.
Keeps the team synchronized on glucose assessment, treatment timing, extraction, and reassessment priorities with little wasted discussion.
Delivers a clinically sharp handoff that tells the full story of initial presentation, diabetic context, response to dextrose, and residual concerns.

title: Pressure in the Chest
scenarioIntro: This scenario focuses on chest pain assessment, ACS recognition, 12-lead ECG interpretation, medication decisions including ASA and nitroglycerin, reassessment after treatment, and transport decision-making. The patient may improve slightly with treatment but remains high risk throughout the call.

dispatchNotes: Male chest pain, conscious and breathing
callInformation.crewNotes: Consider ACS, obtain 12-lead early, and monitor blood pressure closely if nitroglycerin is given.

incidentNarrative: Crew is dispatched for a 58-year-old male with chest pain. On arrival, patient is seated on the couch leaning forward slightly and appears uncomfortable. He states the pain started while mowing the lawn about 30 minutes ago and has not gone away. He stopped working, came inside, and sat down, but the pain remained and he began to feel sweaty and nauseated. His wife called 911 when he said the pressure felt different from anything he had experienced before.
patientPresentation: Patient is alert but uncomfortable, holding the center of his chest. Skin is pale and slightly diaphoretic. He appears anxious and is breathing a little more quickly than normal but is able to speak in full sentences.

sceneArrival.sceneDescription: Private residence living room, patient seated on couch
sceneArrival.accessIssues: No access issues

firstImpression.generalAppearance: Middle-aged male in mild to moderate distress, pale and diaphoretic
firstImpression.apparentSeverity: High-risk cardiac presentation requiring prompt assessment and action
firstImpression.positionFound: Seated leaning slightly forward on couch

initialAssessment.circulation: Radial pulse present, regular, slightly rapid
initialAssessment.generalImpression: High-risk ACS presentation in a middle-aged male
immediatePriorities: Monitor blood pressure before any nitroglycerin

bystanderInformation: Wife called 911 when patient described the pain as unlike anything before
sceneContextClues: Previous lawn work without symptoms not mentioned

secondaryAssessment.generalAppearance: Remains pale and uncomfortable despite modest reduction in pain after treatment
secondaryAssessment.breathing: Stable and non-laboured throughout
secondaryAssessment.circulation: Blood pressure responds to nitroglycerin; trend matters more than a single post-treatment reading
keyFindings: ST elevation in inferior leads on 12-lead
Partial pain relief after first nitroglycerin dose
missedIfNotAssessed: Persistent ECG changes despite symptom improvement
evolvingFindings: Patient remains STEMI risk regardless of pain score; transport urgency does not decrease with modest improvement

additionalAssessments: Right-sided leads if inferior STEMI suspected

transportConsiderations: Rapid transport to cardiac-capable facility
Avoid prolonged on-scene time for a STEMI pattern
ongoingCare: Repeat vital signs after any medication
transportPhase.handoffConsiderations: Communicate exertional onset, ECG findings, medication administered with BP before and after dosing, and current hemodynamic status.

opqrst.onset: Sudden onset about 30 minutes ago while mowing the lawn
opqrst.provocation: Worse with exertion, no significant relief with rest
opqrst.radiation: Into the left arm and jaw

sample.signsAndSymptoms: Central chest pressure, diaphoresis, nausea, mild shortness of breath
sample.eventsLeadingUp: Was mowing the lawn when chest pain began suddenly and persisted despite sitting down to rest

physicalExam.generalAppearance: Middle-aged male in mild to moderate distress due to chest pain
physicalExam.breathing: Slightly increased rate, non-laboured, clear breath sounds bilaterally
physicalExam.circulation: Radial pulse present, regular, slightly rapid

firstSet.context: Initial assessment on scene before any treatment

secondSet.context: After ASA and 12-lead acquisition, before nitroglycerin

additionalSets.context: After one nitroglycerin dose with appropriate blood pressure monitoring
After repeated nitroglycerin given without careful reassessment

withProperTreatment: ASA is administered early and a 12-lead is obtained promptly, confirming inferior ST elevation.
Nitroglycerin is considered carefully with blood pressure confirmed adequate before each dose.
Pain improves modestly but the patient remains pale and monitored closely throughout transport.
withoutProperTreatment: If ASA is not given and the ECG is delayed, the patient remains in pain with worsening diaphoresis and nausea.
Ongoing ischemia without intervention increases the risk of dysrhythmia or hemodynamic deterioration.
Failure to recognize ST elevation keeps the crew from communicating the correct hospital pre-alert.
withIncorrectTreatment: If nitroglycerin is repeated without rechecking blood pressure or recognizing the inferior STEMI pattern, the patient becomes hypotensive and lightheaded.
A drop in blood pressure from preload reduction in an inferior STEMI may worsen right ventricular compromise.
Apparent pain improvement after nitro masks worsening hemodynamic status if reassessment is skipped.

expectedTreatment: Perform a primary assessment and focused chest pain assessment
Administer ASA if indicated and not contraindicated
Administer nitroglycerin only if indicated and blood pressure permits
Reassess blood pressure, pain, and patient appearance after nitroglycerin
Monitor ECG and vital signs continuously
Prioritize rapid transport to an appropriate facility
protocolNotes: Chest pain suggestive of ACS should prompt early 12-lead acquisition
ASA should be administered early unless contraindicated per ALS PCS
Nitroglycerin requires adequate blood pressure and reassessment after each dose
Inferior STEMI patterns should increase caution with nitroglycerin due to preload dependency
Oxygen is not automatic when SpO2 is adequate
Pain improvement does not reduce transport urgency in a confirmed STEMI
teachersPoints: This is a time-sensitive ACS recognition and transport call, not just a chest pain complaint. A patient can look relatively stable and still be having a STEMI. 12-lead acquisition should happen early enough to change the direction of the call. Inferior STEMIs require more careful thinking about preload, blood pressure, and nitroglycerin. Pain score alone cannot tell you whether the patient is improving or deteriorating. Trend blood pressure and patient appearance after every medication dose.
clinicalReasoning: This presentation is most consistent with an inferior STEMI based on classic ischemic pain features, exertional onset, radiation to the left arm and jaw, diaphoresis, nausea, and ST elevation on ECG. The most dangerous pitfall is repeated nitroglycerin dosing without recognizing that inferior STEMI patients may be preload-dependent due to right ventricular involvement, making hypotension a real risk after aggressive vasodilation. Pain improvement after one dose does not eliminate the need for continued monitoring and caution. The differential includes musculoskeletal or esophageal causes, but the constellation of exertional onset, pressure quality, radiation, diaphoresis, and ECG changes strongly supports ACS. Ontario BLS/ALS PCS compliance is required throughout.
scenarioRationale: This scenario is designed to teach chest pain assessment, early ECG use, ASA administration, thoughtful nitroglycerin use, and the importance of reassessment after treatment. It reinforces that STEMI care is not only about identifying the rhythm but about understanding how treatment and transport decisions change patient risk.
learningObjectives: Perform a complete chest pain assessment using OPQRST and SAMPLE
Recognize ACS features and ECG findings consistent with STEMI
Administer ASA appropriately per protocol
Administer nitroglycerin thoughtfully with blood pressure monitoring before each dose
Reassess after treatment and during transport
Understand the importance of rapid transport in STEMI care
selfReflectionPrompts: What historical and presentation clues made ACS more likely in this patient?
How did the ECG influence your treatment and transport decisions?
What changed after nitroglycerin, and how did that affect your thinking?
Why is an inferior STEMI pattern a reason to be more cautious with nitroglycerin?
What findings would make you more cautious about repeating a second nitroglycerin dose?

3: Recognizes chest pain as concerning but is slow to treat it as a probable ACS call.
Obtains ECG late or does not fully appreciate the significance of an inferior STEMI pattern.
Underestimates transport urgency because the patient is still talking and appears to be perfusing adequately.
5: Recognizes ACS as the likely cause early and reframes the call accordingly.
Obtains ECG promptly and identifies the need for rapid transport to a cardiac-capable facility.
Maintains awareness of blood pressure trend, patient appearance, and deterioration risk throughout.
7: Immediately reframes the call as a high-risk STEMI rather than a generic chest pain complaint.
Anticipates hypotension risk from nitroglycerin in an inferior STEMI and monitors proactively before each dose.
Maintains excellent situational awareness of treatment effect, hemodynamic trend, and transport priority without breaking care flow.

3: Obtains a partial chest pain history but misses high-value details such as exertional onset, radiation, or associated symptoms.
Asks some SAMPLE questions but leaves important medication and cardiac history gaps.
Does not use history findings effectively to sharpen ACS suspicion or guide ECG interpretation.
5: Obtains a clear OPQRST and SAMPLE history that captures the key ACS features.
Identifies exertional onset, radiation to arm and jaw, diaphoresis, and nausea appropriately.
Uses history findings to support ECG interpretation, medication decisions, and transport urgency.
7: Uses history gathering efficiently to strongly support ACS suspicion before the ECG is even acquired.
Identifies high-value risk features and the metoprolol and atorvastatin context without wasting time.
Integrates history seamlessly into treatment planning, nitroglycerin caution reasoning, and pre-alert communication.

3: Performs a basic assessment but leaves important gaps in cardiac-focused findings.
Obtains ECG or vitals but does not fully integrate them into the clinical picture.
Reassessment after nitroglycerin is delayed or limited to asking about pain without checking blood pressure.
5: Performs a focused and organized ACS assessment including 12-lead acquisition and baseline vital signs.
Monitors vitals appropriately and reassesses pain, blood pressure, and patient appearance after treatment.
Recognizes that persistent ECG changes matter even when pain scores improve slightly.
7: Builds a coherent ACS assessment from history, appearance, ECG, and serial vital sign trends.
Recognizes that pain score alone does not indicate improvement and uses objective data to guide decisions.
Uses serial reassessment including BP after each medication dose to catch evolving hemodynamic risk early.

3: Recognizes the need for treatment but is hesitant or delayed in acting on ACS suspicion.
Gives nitroglycerin with limited or absent reassessment of blood pressure afterward.
Delays the transport decision or under-uses the ECG findings to shape care priority.
5: Administers ASA appropriately and uses nitroglycerin thoughtfully with blood pressure checks.
Reassesses symptom response and hemodynamics after each medication dose.
Makes an appropriate transport decision based on STEMI risk without unnecessary on-scene prolongation.
7: Prioritizes ECG acquisition, ASA, and rapid transport early without wasting on-scene time.
Uses the inferior STEMI pattern and blood pressure trend to apply careful, well-reasoned nitroglycerin decisions.
Balances treatment, reassessment, and transport with strong integrated clinical judgment throughout the call.

3: Provides basic explanations but leaves the patient and wife without a clear understanding of what is happening.
Partner coordination during medication and ECG tasks is present but unstructured.
Handover is incomplete and does not clearly communicate the ECG findings, medications given, or treatment response.
5: Communicates clearly with the patient and partner during assessment, treatment, and transport.
Explains ASA, nitroglycerin, and transport urgency in a way the patient understands.
Delivers an organized handover that includes ECG findings, medications, blood pressure responses, and current stability.
7: Uses calm, confidence-building communication throughout a high-stakes cardiac call.
Keeps the patient informed, manages the wife's anxiety without slowing care, and coordinates team actions precisely.
Delivers a concise, clinical STEMI handover that communicates the full story including ECG pattern, medications, and hemodynamic trend.

title: Too Hot to Hold Still
scenarioIntro: This scenario focuses on pediatric assessment during and after a febrile seizure. It emphasizes seizure safety, rapid post-ictal reassessment, pediatric temperature interpretation, focused history from caregivers, recognition of red flags for serious infection, and transport planning even after apparent recovery.

dispatchNotes: 3-year-old actively seizing, fever reported by parent
callInformation.crewNotes: Prioritize seizure safety, ABC reassessment after seizure stops, and pediatric fever red flags.

incidentNarrative: Crew is dispatched for a 3-year-old child with seizure activity. On arrival, the seizure has just stopped after approximately 2 minutes per parent report. The child is post-ictal, drowsy, and febrile. Parents report two days of upper respiratory symptoms with reduced oral intake and worsening fever this evening. No known trauma occurred during the event. The family is highly anxious and repeatedly asks if the child is dying.
patientPresentation: Child is lying on the bed, flushed, sweaty, and drowsy but breathing spontaneously with a patent airway. There is no ongoing tonic-clonic movement on crew arrival. Parents are distressed and speaking over each other.

sceneArrival.sceneDescription: Child found on a bed in a warm bedroom with parents at bedside
sceneArrival.accessIssues: Narrow hallway slows stretcher movement
sceneArrival.bystandersPresent: Both parents present and highly anxious

firstImpression.generalAppearance: Pediatric patient post-seizure and febrile with spontaneous breathing
firstImpression.apparentSeverity: Potentially serious pediatric presentation requiring urgent reassessment and transport
firstImpression.positionFound: Supine on bed, side-turned by parent

initialAssessment.breathing: Spontaneous, slightly tachypneic, no severe distress
initialAssessment.circulation: Peripheral pulses present, warm peripheries, tachycardic
initialAssessment.disability: Post-ictal drowsiness, responds to painful stimulus then voice
initialAssessment.exposure: No trauma identified, febrile on touch
initialAssessment.generalImpression: Likely febrile seizure with current post-ictal state; must rule out concerning causes
immediatePriorities: Rapid full reassessment after post-ictal phase

withoutProperTreatment: If post-ictal status is mistaken for full recovery, concerning neurologic signs and trend deterioration may be missed.
Poor reassessment and delayed transport increase risk of unnoticed recurrent seizure or airway compromise.
Inadequate caregiver history leads to incomplete red-flag screening and weaker handoff quality.
withIncorrectTreatment: If the child is overstimulated, repeatedly repositioned, or not protected in lateral posture while drowsy, airway risk increases.
If the team anchors on simple fever without seizure-focused reassessment, persistent altered consciousness may be under-recognized.
If recurrence planning is absent, a second seizure during transport causes delayed response and avoidable hypoxia.

expectedTreatment: Perform primary pediatric assessment with seizure safety and airway protection
Position appropriately during post-ictal phase and minimize stimulation
Obtain early pediatric vitals including temperature and BGL
Gather focused caregiver timeline including seizure duration and return-to-baseline pattern
Screen for red flags requiring urgent pediatric evaluation
Perform serial neurologic reassessment during transport
Prepare for recurrent seizure and prioritize timely transport
protocolNotes: Pediatric seizure with fever requires continuous reassessment even if convulsions have stopped
Post-ictal drowsiness should improve over time; persistent depression of consciousness is concerning
Airway positioning and minimal stimulation are key early priorities
BGL check helps exclude hypoglycemia as a seizure contributor
Fever context supports febrile seizure but does not rule out serious infection
Rapid transport and high-quality handoff are required for definitive pediatric evaluation
teachersPoints: A resolved seizure is not a resolved call; the post-ictal trend is the core teaching point. Pediatric scenes can become history-poor unless one provider deliberately structures caregiver questioning. Fever can explain a seizure, but red-flag screening still matters every time. Airway risk in a drowsy child is often more dangerous than dramatic but brief convulsive activity. Trend-based reassessment is what separates safe pediatric care from checkbox care.
clinicalReasoning: This presentation is most consistent with a febrile seizure given age, acute generalized seizure history, concurrent febrile viral illness, and post-ictal recovery trend without immediate focal deficits. The key risk is premature reassurance after convulsions stop; the safer approach is serial neurologic and airway reassessment with transport for full pediatric evaluation. Differential concerns include CNS infection, metabolic causes, toxic ingestion, and first-time epilepsy, especially if mental status fails to improve, recurrent seizures occur, or focal findings appear. Ontario BLS/ALS PCS compliance is required throughout.
scenarioRationale: This scenario is designed to teach pediatric seizure management beyond the convulsive phase, focusing on airway protection, trend recognition, caregiver history quality, and transport urgency despite partial improvement. It reinforces that febrile seizure calls require structured reassessment and disciplined handoff, not reassurance based on temporary stabilization.
learningObjectives: Demonstrate safe pediatric seizure and post-ictal management
Perform focused caregiver history gathering with a clear timeline
Use serial pediatric reassessment to track recovery versus deterioration
Recognize red flags that require urgent escalation and transport
Communicate effectively with distressed caregivers while preserving care tempo

title: The Bite of Dessert
scenarioIntro: This scenario focuses on recognition and management of anaphylaxis, emphasizing early epinephrine, adjunctive diphenhydramine, bronchodilator support when bronchospasm persists, serial reassessment, and rapid transport. It reinforces that temporary improvement after first-line treatment does not remove deterioration risk.

dispatchNotes: Teenager with allergic reaction, trouble breathing, facial swelling
callInformation.crewNotes: Treat as anaphylaxis if multisystem allergic signs are present; epinephrine early, reassess airway and breathing continuously.

incidentNarrative: Crew is dispatched to a banquet hall where a 16-year-old developed sudden hives, lip swelling, throat tightness, and wheezing shortly after eating dessert at a buffet. A friend reports known peanut allergy and says the patient may have accidentally consumed peanut-containing sauce. On arrival, the patient is anxious, speaking in short phrases, scratching diffusely, and appears increasingly breathless. No epinephrine auto-injector was used before EMS arrival because it could not be found.
patientPresentation: Teenager seated upright, visibly distressed with facial flushing, perioral swelling, diffuse urticaria, hoarse voice, and audible expiratory wheeze. Able to answer but only in short phrases. Bystanders are clustered close and repeatedly interrupt care.

sceneArrival.bystandersPresent: Friends and staff present; one parent arrives during care

firstImpression.apparentSeverity: Time-critical anaphylaxis requiring immediate medication and close trend monitoring

initialAssessment.generalImpression: Anaphylaxis with respiratory and cutaneous involvement, high risk of rapid deterioration

Prepare for repeat epinephrine if symptoms worsen
Keep patient in monitored position of comfort

transportPhase.handoffConsiderations: Communicate exposure timeline, symptoms by system, epinephrine timing and response, diphenhydramine administration, bronchodilator response, and any recurrence concerns.

opqrst.provocation: Worsened with ongoing reaction progression; no non-allergic trigger identified
opqrst.quality: Throat tightness, chest tightness, itch, and breathing difficulty
opqrst.severity: Severe distress initially, partial improvement after treatment
opqrst.time: Rapid escalation over approximately 10 to 15 minutes before EMS arrival

sample.signsAndSymptoms: Hives, lip swelling, throat tightness, wheeze, dyspnea, anxiety
sample.lastOralIntake: Buffet dinner and dessert just before symptom onset
sample.eventsLeadingUp: Accidental likely peanut exposure followed by rapidly progressive multisystem allergic symptoms

physicalExam.generalAppearance: Teen in acute allergic distress with visible rash and facial swelling
physicalExam.airway: Patent but hoarse voice and swelling raise concern
physicalExam.breathing: Diffuse expiratory wheeze, tachypnea, moderate respiratory effort
physicalExam.headNeck: Perioral and lip edema, no trauma

secondSet.context: After first epinephrine dose and immediate reassessment

additionalSets.context: After diphenhydramine and salbutamol for persistent wheeze
If recurrence during transport

withProperTreatment: Prompt IM epinephrine produces partial early improvement in airway symptoms and respiratory effort.
Diphenhydramine is given as an adjunct and salbutamol is used when wheeze persists, improving bronchospasm further.
Patient remains at risk of rebound symptoms but reaches ED in a more stable state with clear treatment timeline.
withoutProperTreatment: If epinephrine is delayed, airway swelling and bronchospasm progress rapidly with worsening oxygenation.
Voice changes may progress to severe airway compromise and perfusion can decline as the reaction evolves.
Late treatment increases risk of hypotension, severe respiratory failure, and critical deterioration during transport.
withIncorrectTreatment: If antihistamine is used as first-line while epinephrine is delayed, transient itch relief may mask worsening airway risk.
If persistent wheeze is not treated after epinephrine, ongoing bronchospasm maintains respiratory distress.
If early improvement is mistaken for resolution, recurrence may be missed until the patient is unstable.

expectedTreatment: Recognize anaphylaxis using multisystem allergic findings
Administer IM epinephrine early as first-line treatment
Provide oxygen and continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring
Administer diphenhydramine as adjunct per protocol
Use salbutamol when bronchospasm and wheeze persist
Perform frequent reassessment for airway, breathing, and perfusion changes
Prepare for repeat epinephrine if deterioration recurs
protocolNotes: Epinephrine is first-line treatment in anaphylaxis and should not be delayed
Diphenhydramine is adjunctive and not a substitute for epinephrine
Persistent wheeze after epinephrine can be treated with salbutamol as indicated
Serial reassessment is required because rebound or biphasic deterioration can occur
Early apparent improvement does not remove the need for urgent transport and monitoring
teachersPoints: The most common dangerous error is treating anaphylaxis like a mild allergy and delaying epinephrine. Adjunct medications can support care, but they cannot replace first-line epinephrine in a multisystem reaction. Persistent wheeze after epinephrine should trigger bronchodilator thinking rather than watchful delay. Trend airway signs and voice quality, not just rash severity, when deciding urgency. Temporary improvement should increase vigilance for recurrence, not lower it.
clinicalReasoning: This patient has anaphylaxis based on rapid onset after likely allergen exposure with multisystem involvement: cutaneous findings, upper airway symptoms, and lower respiratory bronchospasm. Early IM epinephrine is the key intervention because delayed dosing increases risk of airway compromise and hemodynamic collapse. Diphenhydramine is an adjunct for histamine-mediated symptoms but is not first-line for life-threatening physiology. Because wheeze persists after epinephrine, salbutamol is appropriate as supportive therapy for bronchospasm. Continuous reassessment is essential due to recurrence risk despite initial improvement. Ontario BLS/ALS PCS compliance is required throughout.
scenarioRationale: This scenario is designed to teach high-stakes anaphylaxis management in a crowded environment, emphasizing medication prioritization and reassessment discipline. It reinforces epinephrine-first decision making, proper adjunct use, and the need to maintain transport urgency even when early response looks reassuring.
learningObjectives: Recognize anaphylaxis using rapid onset and multisystem criteria
Prioritize epinephrine as first-line treatment
Use diphenhydramine appropriately as an adjunct
Treat persistent bronchospasm with salbutamol when indicated
Perform trend-based reassessment and recurrence planning during transport
selfReflectionPrompts: What findings made this anaphylaxis rather than a simple allergic reaction?
How quickly did you prioritize epinephrine, and what might have delayed you?
When is diphenhydramine useful and what can it not do in anaphylaxis?
What reassessment findings justified salbutamol in this case?
How did you protect against overconfidence after initial improvement?

7: Builds an integrated trend from exposure history, serial respiratory findings, hemodynamics, and treatment response.
Detects incomplete response early and adjusts care before obvious deterioration.
Maintains high-fidelity reassessment throughout movement and transport without losing diagnostic clarity.

3: Delays epinephrine while attempting lower-impact interventions first.
Uses adjunct medications without clear prioritization of first-line anaphylaxis care.
Underestimates recurrence risk and relaxes monitoring too early after partial improvement.
5: Prioritizes epinephrine early and uses adjunctive diphenhydramine appropriately.
Adds salbutamol when persistent bronchospasm remains clinically relevant after first-line treatment.

7: Executes decisive epinephrine-first management with strong sequencing and minimal delay.
Uses adjuncts judiciously while continuously re-evaluating airway and perfusion risk.

3: Provides limited explanation of urgency, causing confusion about medication choices and transport necessity.
Team tasking is inconsistent during medication delivery and reassessment cycles.
